sentences.text: Yes.
I could tell you the exact beach, and the name of the seaside.
It was Lion-sur-Mer, and the beach was Sword Beach.
People who landed there would know all about it.
Sword, that was the name of the actual beach.
And the actual Village of a seaside town was Lion-- L-I-O-N-- Lion-sur-Mer.
The large town was--
--Caen.
That was about five miles in.
But it was so well defended our troops couldn't go in.
For about a month we were outside.
And when, eventually, our troops took Caen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish troops in the area held a service in a tent.
And to their surprise, civilians attended.
In hiding, oh yes.
In hiding.
As far as I know, yes.
Yes.
He was the chaplain.
He was the chaplain.
No.
When the troops moved on, we followed them.
Wherever they went, we went with them.
Well, the work we did, we were in a special unit.
Specially formed.
I'll give you an idea.
Oh, further back to a field hospital.
Yes.
FDS, field dressing station.
British, yes.
No, there weren't any Americans in our area.
It was all British.
But we had a few Germans.
They were treated just the same.
Well, when they were OK, it's on to a hospital or prison camp.
Not much.
But what we did notice, that the British troops were friendly with the Germans.
They showed each other photographs of their family.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
We heard about it, yes.
No, no.
The others weren't interested, no.
They had their own worries, yes.
I know why they left Lithuania.
No, but the people who were left behind.
No.
No.
Disappeared altogether.
We didn't know, no.
See, my father's [INAUDIBLE], as a boy he had to join the Army.
He didn't.
They got married, in Kovna, and off they went.
But there were still a lot of other relatives back in Europe.
There was still a lot of other family back in Europe.
Oh yes, oh yes.
Near Paris.
When Paris was taken we were allowed in to have a look.
All American troops there, only.
They didn't want the English troops there.
But our officer let us go in, just to have a look around at Paris.
The French girls.
[LAUGHTER]
They made a [INAUDIBLE] of us.
Pardon?
No, no, no, no.
We were moving all the time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From there, we crossed the border into Holland.
We were stationed in Holland.
By the river Maas.
I don't' know if you know it.
It's part of the Rhine.
We were stationed there quite a long time.
Nijmegen, there was fighting there.
We were outside there.
And--
No.
No.
Not actual casualties.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, soldiers who were sick, and so on.
We were like a followup unit.
And from Belgium we were in Holland.
I told you that.
Yes, all the time.
We met quite a few Dutch people, Jewish, who were in hiding.
Yes.
They were pleased to see me, being Jewish.
Yes.
No, no.
See, when you're in the Army, you're tied.
You can't go wherever you want to.
You go where you're told.
And you can't go walking about it now.
And then--
Oh, yes, we got letters.
But we weren't allowed to mention any names.
No.
No, places, nothing like that.
Well, in the early stage of the war they were evacuated to Cambridge.
Out of the bombing area.
Then later on, it eased up, they came back.
And we lived at home.
Well, they did, not me.
In the Army?
On the ground.
I never slept in a bed the whole time I was in the Army.
No.
We were on the move.
If not, all we had was a blanket and a ground sheet, and you slept on that.
And glad of it.
All our rations came in tins until our field unit got moving.
And they made it-- made-- they did cooking, yes.
Yes.
Well, we crossed over into Germany.
And the war apparently was finishing.
We never-- we were never in a town.
Always in villages, or outside.
We finished up in Germany.
And war had just finished.
I think it was VE-Day.
Round about that time.
There was victory in Europe.
And we were in Germany.
And we were celebrating, if you can call it that.
About 3 o'clock in the morning, we were sleeping on the ground in the house.
We were waken-- woken up-- woken up, and we were told to move on, because the tank car in front of us, they discovered a concentration camp.
And there were a lot of typhus patients there-- men.
This was Sandbostel.
And they couldn't cope with that.
They were tank corps.
The weren't a military-- uh, what do you call it?
A medical unit-- to help out.
So we were sent there, 3 o'clock in the morning.
We came across this camp.
It's a hutted camp.
We were told there was about 4,000 men there-- no women.
Most of them had typhus.
They were dying like flies.
Which way could we help?
Well, they decided that every patient-- I use the term patient-- had to be cleansed-- washed.
The uniform-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it?
Clothes they wore had to be destroyed.
See, typhus is spread by a louse-- lice.
And it's very infectious.
They had to be burned.
And each man had to be washed, and had to be sprayed with anti-louse powder-- that's to kill the lice.
A-L-- anti-louse powder.
Everybody came-- who came into the camp had to be deloused.
Even visitors.
We had to be deloused.
Before we came amongst the people there, we had to be sprayed-- sprayed with this powder.
No.
There was about 25 of us.
That's right, very special part of the unit.
And we had to watch-- but we couldn't do all that work, there were so many people there.
What our commandant did, he sent a message to the nearest town-- I forget.
Bremen, yes, that's right, Bremen.
He wanted 600 women to work in a hospital.
An order.
See, we were victors.
He had to find 600 women and girls to come to work in the hospital.
They were brought out to our camp, and there they allocated duties.
The strong ones had to dig graves.
The others had to the washing.
And each one, as they came out of the hutted camp-- it was a filthy space, it was.
I had to look inside later on.
Had to be placed on a table-- and we supervised that they did their work properly.
Everyone had to be washed down and sprayed with powder that killed lice.
Pardon?
Typhus, as far as we knew.
There was doctors there, but I didn't know about them.
Each one was brought in and cleansed.
And when they were cleansed, and if they didn't need any treatment, they were shifted elsewhere.
We don't know.
Away from the camp.
Sandbostel.
S-A-N-D-B-O-S-T-E-L, as far as I know.
No.
It was like a staging camp where they brought these inmates-- I use that term, inmates-- from the extermination camps up north.
And there they were allowed to remain there, before they were shifted, further still.
No.
No.
No.
And as they brought each one in, I stood by the bed, or table, to see that they did their work properly.
And as they came in, each one, I whispered to each one.
You know what I whispered?
[YIDDISH].
Yes.
But later on I'll tell you, there was a special one.
Made me cry.
After a time they brought one in, a man, in his, about, 50s.
In his 50s.
And there was no room where they'd do the cleansing.
He was on a stretcher on the grass.
And I knelt down beside him, and I said, [YIDDISH].
He was half stunned beforehand.
He didn't know where he was.
As soon as I said that, he looked up at me.
He said, [YIDDISH]?
I said, yes.
He bent down and kissed my feet.
And who was he?
Where did he come from, that man?
I said, where from?
He said, Hungary.
He said, [YIDDISH]-- Will I live?
I said, you'll live, we don't know, we'll try.
And that's all.
He was sent out, but I don't know.
Don't know.
Don't know.
You see, they were out of our hands.
Once they reached that stage, they were out of our hands.
Yes.
There was one, a young-- youngish man, he was about 35.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
About 35.
He spoke English.
I said, where from?
Lithuania.
He came from Kovna.
He was a doctor.
He had a medical degree, a real doctor.
And he knew I spoke-- spoke English.
And he said to me, could I do him a favor?
He's got cousins in London in the fur trade.
Could I get in touch with them, and tell them that he's still alive?
I says, I can't contact London at all.
It's out of the question.
We're in the Army.
I said, what I'll do, I'll write to my wife and tell her this man has cousins in London who are in the fur trade, mention their name.
And just tell them that this doctor is still alive.
Levin.
Levin, I think, yes.
I do not remember.
I forgot.
It's over 50 years ago.
I don't know.
But what my wife did, she looked up in the telephone directory of all of the furriers of this particular name.
And she found the cousin, and she contacted him, and they wrote.
She told me this, that this man had a cousin-- a sister in Canada.
And they wrote to the sister, to say that her brother's still alive.
That's all I know.
Maybe he got to Canada.
Perhaps he did.
I don't know.
See, once they left us, it was a different story.
It didn't concern us any more.
We did our job, and off they went.
I don't know.
See, we did our job, and we were finished.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Another little incident, they brought in two little boys, Jewish boys, they must have been about 10 years old.
And I spoke Yiddish to them.
And they said, could I help him-- help them?
With what?
They were twins.
They'd been-- two boys had been together all the time.
Now, when they go away from our-- our camp, would I see they were kept together?
I says, I'll try my best, I can't tell.
Once you're finished with us, I'll speak to the officer.
I'll tell him to keep the two boys together.
And that's all you know about it.
That's all we know.
We weren't allowed to have cameras.
No.
Because in case we were captured, and the Germans-- well, knew exactly where we were.
We weren't allowed to keep-- keep a diary.
No.
Yes.
Yes.
No, I saw the German women digging graves.
That's all I saw.
And I walked into one of the camps, it was a hutted.
There was three banks, and they were lying the inmates-- I'm sorry I use that term-- they were lying all on top of each other.
Just in a filthy state, and they had typhus.
And their bowels were working and running.
And what did they do?
They couldn't get off the camp.
Wherever they were, they used it.
It was all over the floor, yes.
And were there rabbis there, to-- to perform--
No, no.
And the graves weren't marked in any way--
No.
All we saw was digging the graves, [BACKGROUND NOISE] but that's about all.
We don't know.
See, we were in a field unit, with no facilities for anything like that.
Pardon?
No.
The Americans were a bit more down south.
We were north.
Going towards Hamburg, and so on.
The women, yes.
What we had to do, as they washed each one down, all they were-- the inmates-- skin and bone.
You saw their chest, like a bird cage covered with skin.
They [INAUDIBLE] thin.
And we had to demonstrate to the German women, see, this man is a normal man-- he was.
Look at him now, a skeleton.
Thank Hitler for this.
And how did they respond to that?
Well, most of them say they didn't know.
Could be true, I don't know.
But we had one girl there, she was about 16, and she was a very good-- a German girl-- very good worker, very conscientious.
And when she saw these men, she told us she belonged to the Hitler Youth.
She said you couldn't help herself, because they enjoy themselves.
All the girls and boys, it was good.
But what she did, she tore her card up.
And because you could speak Yiddish--
Yes.
That's right, yes.
Not as far as I know.
It was only like, hardly a village, just a few houses there.
They were sorry.
Mm-hmm.
Because it was brought home to us in actual facts.
Up to now it was just theory-- hearsay.
But now we saw it.
I felt sad.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Thinking that my people came from Lithuania, and if they didn't get married, they would have been there, yes.
I used to think about it a lot.
But it's only thoughts, yes.
It was brought home to me.
No, no.
They all disappeared.
No.
No, no.
Yes.
That's right.
Well, the unit that I was with was an invasion unit, trained for invasion.
Now, the war in Japan was still on.
But in Europe it was finished.
They-- the government decided to bring our unit back to England, to fit us out with new clothes for-- for jungle warfare.
And we were going for the invasion of the Japanese landings.
A town, yes.
We were there, and we were going to be brought back to England to be fitted out.
But then the bomb dropped in Japan.
Yes.
And they brought us back to Germany again.
So I was saved for that.
No, no.
We were only in villages.
A place called [?
Mettigan, ?] I think.
A place you never heard of.
I can't remember.
In fact, one time we were stationed in a castle, a schloss.
The baron still lived there, but he was pro-British, so they say.
We were allowed to live there.
Not any best rooms, not a ballroom.
In the out houses.
Let's see, we all had-- they worked your-- they worked out the time you were going out by your age and in the amount of service you did in the forces.
I was number 29.
But I forget when it was.
'46, yeah.
No.
No.
We were just [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah.
Went back to London, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, people, I mean, naturally.
Yes, yes.
Yes, that's right.
Yes, but I couldn't stop in the fur trade.
Do you know why?
I was used to the outdoor life.
Living outdoors all the time.
And I couldn't go back into a factory.
I couldn't stand in it.
So I took a job selling.
Selling, and I could be outside, that's right.
And--
And that's how I finished up.
Working for [?
Costetna. ?]
